flashcards.front: Define Transmission Media in computer networks and differentiate between guided and unguided media.
flashcards.back: Transmission media is the physical pathway through which data is transmitted from sender to receiver. Guided media (bounded) uses physical cables (twisted pair, coaxial, fiber) to direct signals, while unguided media (unbounded) transmits signals wirelessly through air, vacuum, or water using electromagnetic waves.

flashcards.front: Compare Unshielded Twisted Pair (UTP) and Shielded Twisted Pair (STP) cables, including their categories and typical applications.
flashcards.back: UTP cables lack shielding, are cheaper and easier to install, but susceptible to EMI; categories include Cat5e (100MHz, 1Gbps), Cat6 (250MHz, 10Gbps). STP cables have metallic shielding reducing EMI, used in environments with high interference, but are costlier and harder to install. Both used in LANs, telephone systems, and Ethernet networks.

flashcards.front: Describe the structure and types of coaxial cable, including their bandwidth and common applications.
flashcards.back: Coaxial cable has a central copper conductor, insulating layer, braided metal shield, and outer jacket—providing excellent EMI shielding. Two types: Thinnet (10Base2, RG-58, 185m max, 10Mbps) used for thin Ethernet; Thicknet (10Base5, RG-8, 500m max, 10Mbps) used as backbone. Also used for cable TV (RG-6) and cable internet.

flashcards.front: Explain the working principle of fiber optic cables and list their advantages over copper cables.
flashcards.back: Fiber optic cables transmit data as light pulses through total internal reflection in glass/plastic fibers. Advantages: extremely high bandwidth (up to 100Gbps+), low attenuation (100-200km repeaters), immunity to EMI, lighter weight, better security (difficult to tap), and no electrical interference. Used in long-distance telecom, internet backbone, and high-speed LANs.

flashcards.front: Differentiate between Single-Mode Fiber (SMF) and Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF) cables.
flashcards.back: SMF has a small core (8-10μm) allowing only one light mode, enabling long distances (up to 100km+) with high bandwidth; used in telecom and cable TV. MMF has a larger core (50-62.5μm) allowing multiple light modes, supporting shorter distances (up to 2km) with lower cost; used in LANs and data centers. SMF uses laser光源, MMF uses LED.

flashcards.front: What are radio waves in unguided transmission media? Explain their propagation modes and applications.
flashcards.back: Radio waves are electromagnetic waves (3Hz-300GHz) propagating through air without physical guide. Propagation modes: ground wave (follows Earth, AM radio), sky wave (reflects from ionosphere, shortwave radio), line-of-sight (direct path, FM radio, TV, Wi-Fi, cellular networks). Used in Wi-Fi (2.4/5GHz), Bluetooth, cellular mobile communications, and broadcast radio.

flashcards.front: Explain microwave transmission in computer networks, including frequency bands and advantages.
flashcards.back: Microwave transmission uses high-frequency electromagnetic waves (1GHz-300GHz) requiring line-of-sight antennas. Frequencies: L (1-2GHz), S (2-4GHz), C (4-8GHz), Ku (12-18GHz), Ka (26-40GHz). Advantages: high bandwidth, lower cost than fiber for long distances, secure communication, faster deployment. Used in point-to-point links, cellular backhaul, and interconnecting LANs between buildings.

flashcards.front: Describe satellite communication, including orbit types (LEO, MEO, GEO) and applications in networking.
flashcards.back: Satellite communication uses artificial satellites orbiting Earth to transmit signals (microwave/radio). Orbit types: GEO (35,786km, 3 satellites global coverage, TV broadcast), MEO (20,000km, GPS navigation), LEO (500-2000km, low latency, Iridium/Starlink). Applications: TV broadcasting, GPS navigation, weather forecasting, broadband internet (VSAT), and connecting remote locations where cable/fiber is impractical.
